text.content:
Book of the Wider World — p.68-85

Everyone who lives, dies.

When you succumb to injury or illness, or just reach the end of your allotted days, then the Last Door will swing open for you. You will see it. The Lady of Crows will beckon you through. And there on the other side, your soul will join those who have gone before: the honored ancestors, the countless multitudes of the quiet dead.

At least, that’s how it’s meant to go—the natural order of things. But not every soul passes through the Last Door when their time has come. Some linger, others are bound. Some are called back to this world.

Everyone who lives, dies. But not everyone dies the same.

Themes

Choose or roll 1, or 2 and combine.

1d12Theme
1Death personified: the Lady of Crows, the Pale Hunter (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.MCe2YgHD24ptZJrG]{292}), other gods/spirits of death
2The Last Door/brushes with death/ the afterlife/things from beyond
3Graves/funeral rites/grief/mourning
4Death foretold: prophecies, omens, visions, harbingers
5Good death: peaceful, dignified, bravely met, an end of suffering
6Ignoble death: traumatic, desperate, torturous, terrifying
7Untimely death: sudden, surprising, things left unsaid or undone
8Violent death: murder, assassination, execution, battle, suicide
9Mass death: famine, plague, war, cataclysm, collapse
10The fear of death: denial, bargaining, panic, cheating
11The curse of undeath: respite denied, eternal hunger/pain/ loneliness/regret
12Necromancy and death-magic, likely with aid from the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412})

Hooks

  • An abandoned house, out near the Old Wall (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.QMdyuHaPDLL2WHMg]{12}), is said to be haunted.
  • A shade gets called up at the Crossroads (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}) and refuses to go back.
  • A haunted grove near the West Road (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}) is said to hide buried treasure.
  • A necromancer (page 84) comes to town, offering a chance to speak with the dearly departed.

Lore

  • Everyone knows:
  • About the Lady of Crows, the Last Door, and what is said to lie beyond
  • A ghost story or two
  • That dool trees (page 73) are creepy and often haunted
  • That most undead come out only at night
  • One might know:
  • That undead can’t cross an unbroken circle of salt (a pouch full, Value 1) □
  • That a handful of salt will burn most ghosts (1d8 damage, hand, close)
  • That ghosts are bound to a tether (page 77), and what that entails
  • That silver can harm most undead
  • The tale of a prominent haunted site
  • What a given entity is capable of
  • What necromancers are capable of
  • Very few know:
  • The details of an obscure haunting
  • The true nature of the Lady of Crows, the Last Door, the afterlife
  • How necromancy works, exactly

Questions

  • Have you seen the Last Door and the Lady of Crows? What do you recall?
  • What do folks in Stonetop say awaits us, beyond the Last Door? What do they say in/among the __?
  • What do you think awaits us? Why?
  • Who among the dead do you miss most?
  • What ghost story scared you the most as a child?
  • When did you first see someone die?
  • Who was the first person you murdered?

Death’s Door

When the PCs are dying and they make the Death’s Door move (Book I, page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.w4ZAM6a01JmgUtoI]{245}), they see the Last Door and the Lady of Crows. The move prompts them to describe it, but feel free to ask questions.

  • What does the Door look like? Big and grand, small and humble, or what?
  • Is it flung open, or just ajar?
  • What appears to lie beyond?
  • Where does the Lady stand? What does she wear? What adorns her brow?
  • What is she like? Imperious? Friendly? Sad? Smug? Inscrutable? Or what?
  • Does she speak? What is her voice like? What does she say?
  • What detail catches your attention, that you’d never have expected?
  • How much of this will you remember?

As for the afterlife—the land beyond the Last Door—its true nature is a mystery. But it is home to dool spirits (page 83), gwyllgi (page 84), and the shades (page 85) of the dead. Some of those shades, when called, are eager to stay in the land of the living. So for some, it would seem, the afterlife is not a pleasant place at all.

The Lady of Crows

She has many titles. Carrion Queen and Black-Feather Woman. The Kindly One. The Keeper of Secrets. She Who Dances on the Field of Battle. None know her true name, if she even has one. Most call her simply: the Lady of Crows.

Her nature and demeanor are for you and your table to decide, but ask/consider:

  • What is her role? Psychopomp? Judge? Ruler of the afterlife? Or what?
  • What does she want? What pleases her?
  • Is she worshipped as a god? If so, what are her observances (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.ixv4UMnNgm2a0ECI]{184})?
  • What is her origin said to be?
  • What is her relationship to the Pale Hunter (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.MCe2YgHD24ptZJrG]{292})?
  • How do tales of her differ among __? What is notably the same?

Haunted sites

These are places where the undead linger, or where the Last Door stands ajar. If the site itself has yet to be established, combine the terrain in which it’s located with a feature.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.OFmPJUHYWCNK0oYN]{1d12}Feature

1-3A creepy (un)natural landmark (a dool tree, a skull-shaped rock, etc.)
4-5A liminal place (a pass, a bridge, a tunnel, a gate, a crossroad, etc.)
6-8A tomb/barrow/cemetery, left by the locals or their ancestors
9-10An abandoned home/fort/steading
11-12A ruin of the Makers (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.WNfxD0xaxSCvrl2E]{264}) or the ﻿Barrow Builders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}), or a lingering sign of their presence

The PCs are exploring Three Coven Lake (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.uHM6ZyjcWV4QEF22]{438}). They’ve found an ancient Stone Lord (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.UyUHhvUYqOaGERlu]{382}) workshop carved into a bluff above the lake. I think it’s haunted! The nature of the site is established, so I don’t roll for a feature. I roll a 6 for theme (“ignoble death”), a 6 for how long (“for generations”), and a 4 for origins (“the death of a few, intimate and intense”). Because deaths were involved, I roll another 5 for the cause of death (“exposure/cold/thirst/starvation”). I ponder a bit, and come up with this:

A century ago, some adventurers explored these chambers. A great slab of stone fell into place, trapping them in a side chamber. A grate in the ceiling (far out of reach) provided enough fresh air and rainwater to sustain them for several grueling weeks, but one of them murdered and ate his fellows before slowly wasting away.

Once the site’s physical nature is established, pick or roll a theme (page 68), how long it’s been haunted, and the origins of the haunting.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.SWXSlXGllLrVMXgR]{1d12}How long

1Since the days of the Makers (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.WNfxD0xaxSCvrl2E]{264}), at least
2-3Since the Time of Cataclysm (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{413}), when the Makers fell
4-5Since the Barrow Builders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}), after the Makers’ fall
6-7For generations, longer than the living remember
8-9For decades, within living memory
10-11For the past decade at most
12Not long at all, a recent change

His soul lingers as a wraith (page 79), tethered to his bones.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.LS9iKSWxshiCDWLl]{1d12}Origins

1-2The death of many, all at once
3The death of many, over time
4The death of a few, intimate and intense
5-6The death of one, a singular event
7The desecration/violation of a grave
8The arrival of an artifact or tether
9Something about the place itself, attracting/ensnaring dark entities
10A ritual, magic, or artifice: failed or run amok
11A ritual, magic, or artifice: working as intended
12The loss or failure of an artifact/ ward/binding that kept the dead in check

If the origins involved a death or deaths, pick or roll for the cause of death.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.YmXhk6xdyUHLeBus]{1d12}Cause of death

1Old age/infirmity/malady/disease
2-3Suicide/despair/apathy/upset
4-5Exposure/cold/thirst/starvation
6-7Injury/trauma/wounds/poison
8-9Burning/drowning/suffocation
10Falling/accident/weather/disaster
11Exertion/exhaustion/shock/fright
12Magic/an entity/a curse

From there, choose the ﻿ghosts (page 76), revenants (page 80), or other dangers (page 83) that haunt the place, and their tether(s) (page 77) if appropriate. Populate it with artifacts (page 74) tied to ﻿death and the undying, and dangers or discoveries appropriate to the surrounding area and/or whoever dwelt here in the past.

Dool trees

Dool trees often grow in or near haunted sites. A grove stands near the Crossroads (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}), and lone dool trees are often found near Barrow Builder tombs (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}). They are thick and knobby and multi-stemmed. The bark is papery, the color of bleached bone. The Pale Hunter (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.MCe2YgHD24ptZJrG]{292}) shuns them, and they are often home to dool spirits (page 83) if nothing else.

The leaves of a dool tree are deep purple, almost black, shot through with pale white veins. Dried leaves can be steeped to make a bitter tea, which brings on a death-like sleep from which some folk never wake.

Artifacts

Various treasures

  • A clue as to what keeps a ghost from moving on (a letter, a toy, etc.)
  • A wooden idol of the Lady of Crows □ (Value 0, crude), found on a crude altar
  • A vial of lethal poison (1 use, ingested, dangerous, Value 1)
  • A felled dool tree (page 73), its wood valuable to the right person (Value 1 for a □ □ bundle, Value 2 for a cartload)
  • A silver-alloy dagger (Value 2), useful
  • against the undead
  • □ Fine funerary goods, like linens, salts, myrrh, etc. (Value 2)
  • A gold neck torc (Value 3), fitted

around the neck of a murdered corpse

  • A silver sarcophagus, inlaid with runes (immobile, Value 4)

@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.possessions.Item.9O5BRhsUmm42paCf]{A blackened splinter}

crude, magical, fireproof

Undead who see it are drawn to it, or else deeply agitated by its presence. When you touch the splinter to an undead entity, they see the Last Door yawning open before them. Roll 4d6. If the total is greater than the entity’s current HP, they are drawn through the Last Door and leave this world for good. Otherwise, they are transfixed for a few moments before the Door slams shut. Regardless, the splinter vanishes.

@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.possessions.Item.KTpRoKYF9UFhmjRo]{A spindle of fine thread}

magical

A length of fiber, maybe 50 feet long, spun by a ghost from all the hair that ever fell from her daughters’ heads. The thread is light, and fine, and almost unbreakably strong, but it can be handled only by one who has seen the Last Door. Only the edge of a silver blade can cut it. It can be used to bind a ghost fast, if you’re clever and can handle the thread yourself.

A pale wooden sword

  • , close, crude, magical

Carved from a dool tree with a silver knife, and polished with oils infused with moonlight. The grip is worn smooth but the “blade” is chipped and notched, unlikely to stand up to iron or bronze.

When you wield the sword against a manifest undead spirit, you can hurt it (deal damage normally). If you reduce it to 0 HP, the manifest form dissipates but the spirit can reform at its tether normally.

A tattered standard

  • □ , magical

Threadbare, ancient, but remarkably well-preserved. Embroidered with a stylized aurochs whose horns encompass the sun. It fills you with hope and pride to look upon it, as well it should. For this was the standard of the Heol-Tarv, a heroic band who gave their lives in hopeless battle against the Anan Gllo (page 81), securing their people’s escape.

When you unfurl the tattered standard and hold it aloft for all to see, you and your allies are immune to supernatural fear and unshaken by undead or otherwise unnatural foes.

Minor arcana

Pick 1 or have someone roll.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.4dIQx0aXp5MktERG]{1d6}Minor arcanum

1@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Gx7vZa9aCd1eF5gH]{Carvings in a cave} (page 510)
2@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Ja0yDd2dFg4hI8jK]{A grim peat mound} (page 511)
3@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Lc2AfF4fHi6jKaLm]{A tattered mantle} (page 512)
4A scroll & bone flute (page 514)
5@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.Ri8GlL0lNo2pQiRs]{A chain of rusty locks} (page 515)
6@UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.GX9VAA1AEF3FgGHi]{A vein of milky crystal} (page 536)

Major arcana

The following are associated with death and the undying:

  • The @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.d7al3ApmX5jiP5GW]{Demonhide Cloak} (page 544)
  • The Hec’tumel Codex (page 556)
  • The @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.V1Qsr1io0pFhT82S]{Blackwood Fetishes} (page 564)
  • The @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.f4nTtxqREMwt0DWJ]{Twisted Spear} (page 542)

Ghosts

Ghosts are the souls of the dead, those who linger in this world. They vary wildly in personality, motives, and capabilities, but all have the undead and spirit tags, and a move that reflects them manifesting a presence in the world. They are animated by more than biology (+4 HP) and lack vital organs (+3 HP, +1 armor).

Consider the context in which they are encountered. If not already established, pick or roll for their cause of death (page 73). Then pick or roll for what they need to move on, their manifestation, and 1-6 abilities and/or weaknesses. Establish their tether (page 77). Let all these elements inform their story and their stat block.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.KtN0zmAcF0FXdhy2]{1d12}What they need

1-2Kindness/patience/persuasion, a guide to the Last Door
3-4Justice (or vengeance) served
5-6A proper burial
7A reunion with a loved one
8The truth, unearthed/acknowledged
9A task completed; an oath fulfilled
10-11Their tether, destroyed or unmade
12To be put down, like a rabid dog

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.182GKcMN2GubzEdA]{1d12}Manifestation

1Just a whisper/wind/mote of light*
2-3A form of shadow/cold/mist/etc*
4-6A spectral form, ethereal and eerie, but much as they looked in life*
7-9A spectral form, horrible, clearly dead*
10A body made of soil/straw/rags/etc.
11A solid body, horrific and terrifying
12A solid body, seemingly alive but for a few telltale signs

* Harmed only by silver or salt, or some other thematically appropriate substances

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.XXoKo3W6j0bC25dK]{1d12}Ability

1-2Causes eerie, unnatural phenomena
3Feels (feeds on?) emotions/vitality/ sensations of the living
4Can possess certain hosts/vessels
5Unearthly moan/wail/shriek
6Inflicts its pain/feelings on others
7-8Telekinesis (roll 1d6 for scope)
9Nasty/vicious/ghostly attack
10Mind games/phantasms/ compulsions
11-12Can manifest in multiple forms

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.Ev7AB7Izv85kEmvM]{1d12}Weakness

1Afraid of some mundane thing
2Inarticulate, or unable to speak
3Obsessive/predictable behavior
4Easily frustrated/angered/enraged
5Can’t form new memories
6Driven off by singing/prayer/etc.
7-12Shuns/powerless in daylight

Tethers

Ghosts are bound to an object or place. Pick or roll for the tether itself, and how tightly bound the ghost is.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.5HcTARteSQRZ3bbH]{1d12}Tether

1-4Their corpse/remains/bones/hair
5-6The place where they died
7(Part of) their grave/pyre/altar/etc.
8The weapon that killed them
9An item they once cherished
10A prominent place from their life
11-12The focus/source of whatever magic binds them

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.FB03Efnew9Rn9ei0]{1d12}How tightly bound

1-2Can’t leave its immediate presence
3-6Must stay on the premises/site
7-10Can roam a few miles; returns daily
11-12Can wander far and wide, but must return periodically

If a ghost’s manifest form is reduced to 0 HP, they dissipate and return to their tether. They usually won’t manifest before the next sunset; if they do, their max HP are halved.

If a tether is destroyed, then the ghost might… (pick 1)

… fade away, utterly destroyed;
… pass through the Last Door (now, or after some final act);
… devolve into a wraith (page 79);
… possess a corpse, as a pyped (page 82); or
… cling to their current host/form, but fade utterly if it is destroyed.

Decide based on the ghost’s history, power, and relationship to their tether.

The Gwraig Wen
Solitary, spirit, undead, forlorn
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage bare hands d6 w/disadvantage (hand)
Special qualities bound to within a few miles of her bones; can’t form new memories; leaves no footprints
Instinct to drag others into her search
  • Manifest in moonlight as a pale, bedraggled woman
  • Ask for help, insist on it
  • Grow ever more agitated, flickering and vibrating as she does
  • Vent her rage with a deafening wail, d10 damage (near, area, loud, reload, ignores armor)
  • Vanish when no one’s looking
Those who ply the Great Wood (page
200) tell an old tale of a bride, newly
married to a handsome trapper. He failed
to come home one night, and she unwisely
entered the forest to find him, falling into
a deep pond and drowning. Ever since, her
ghost has roamed the woods on moonlit
nights: wet and shivering, desperate and
pleading, certain that her husband is still
out there.
The Ghostly Legion
Horde, spirit, undead, organized,
terrifying, hoarder, violent
HP 10; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage ghostly short sword or spear d6 w/advantage (hand, close)
Special qualities roams far and wide, but tethered to the cave; shuns daylight; can only touch the world through violence; upsetting to/afraid of dogs
Instinct to lift their curse
  • Manifest a spectral form (harmed only by silver, salt, or a loyal dog)
  • Ogle a mortal as they eat or drink
  • Threaten violence, carry through
  • Force a mortal to carry their treasure
They were a band of southern mercenaries,
come north in search of treasure. They
heard tell of a hdour (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.oXGiyvBGsB2GPzQZ]{234}) and his
hoard, trapped him in his cave, and slew
him. Then they divvied up the loot and left.
Only later did they learn why the Hillfolk
fear to kill a hdour. For he had cursed them
all. Cursed their souls to linger after death,
doomed to endless hunger and endless thirst.
Doomed to fear the sun, to be hounded like
the dogs they were. Cursed, indeed, until
every piece of the far-flung loot they stole is
returned to the hdour’s cave.
Specter
Solitary, spirit, undead, stealthy,
terrifying, cruel
HP 23; Armor 1 (lacks vitals)
Damage telekinesis d10+2 (hand, close, near, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities bound to its tether; one or more weaknesses (page 76)
Instinct to torment the living
  • Create eerie environmental effects (bleeding walls, green flames, etc.)
  • Manifest a form of flitting shadow (harmed only by silver or salt)
  • Manifest a nightmarish physical form
  • Speak through an unconscious or catatonic victim
A specter is a soul consumed by hatred and
envy, cruelty and rage. Maybe they were a
sadistic murderer in life, or a victim whose
death was so traumatic that they lash out
at any living thing. Maybe they were a pin-
ing ghost whose loved ones rejected them,
or a fettered soul long- and cruelly-bound,
now set free. Regardless: they are now a
thing of spite and vengeance, wanting only
to punish those with the temerity to live.
Wraith
Solitary, spirit, undead, stealthy,
terrifying
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage life drain d10 (hand, ignores armor)
Special qualities bound to its tether (usually), powerless in daylight
Instinct to feast upon living souls
  • Manifest a form of shadow and cold (harmed only by silver and salt)
  • Hurl itself at the living
  • Suck vitality from its prey
Wraiths are cursed with endless hunger.
They are damaged souls, wounded by dark
magic, another wraith, or the dehumaniz-
ing effects of starvation or addiction. They
are little more than beasts, driven by the
emptiness within, with only the vaguest
sense of who they once were.
Other ghosts might become wraiths when
their tethers are destroyed. If they linger in
the world, they find themselves filled with
gnawing need, and must sustain themselves
on the living. These unbound wraiths are
weaker, but often form packs with others of
their kind (group, vicious instead of stealthy,
13 HP each, d8 damage, afraid of firelight).
When a wraith reduces you to 0 HP and
you survive, your soul is wounded. Reduce
your max HP by 4. Until your soul is
healed, your instinct becomes “Hunger: to
fill the emptiness inside you with excess,”
and should you roll a 6- on Death’s Door,
you die and become a wraith (a monster in
the GM’s control).

Other ghosts

  • A cinderwraith (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.7CqkDvDTCiCWTus2]{166})
  • The Quiet Twins (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eP9K8MXP1j3L6WZk]{396})
  • Phantom riders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{45})
  • Ferocedes Ogran (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Dp02ChmHXxEwVD75]{347})

Revenants

Revenants are the living dead, corpses animated by a soul, a demon, or foul magic. They vary wildly in personality, motives, and capabilities. They are undead, animated by more than biology (+4 HP).

Consider the context in which a revenant is encountered. If not yet established, pick or roll for their cause of death (page 73). Then pick or roll for what they were brought back by, their visage, and 1-6 abilities and/or weaknesses. Let all these elements inform their story and their stat block.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.TrkbJXt5RvAgGk7P]{1d12}Brought back by

1-3A thirst for vengeance
4A longing that transcends death
5Stubbornness/habit/will
6Guilt, like heavy chains
7-8An oath/vow, stronger than iron
9A curse, well-deserved or not
10Their own designs, some dark ritual/deal/preparation
11-12The will or working of another (and maybe roll 1d10 for why/how)

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.N5bZ6Pz3rehNw7tn]{1d12}Visage

1-2Naught but bone, a grinning skull
3-5Tattered, rotting, horrible
6-8Dried out, gaunt, clearly dead; but can pass for living at a distance
9-10Seemingly alive, but clearly dead and horrible in sun/moonlight
11-12Seemingly alive, except for a few telltale signs

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.HBieq23Q4dKRtZnC]{1d12}Ability (2)

1Uncanny speed/grace/prowess
2Unholy strength (forceful, +2 damage)
3-4Unnatural resilience (+4 HP, +4 armor except vs. silver or fire)
5-6Can regenerate/reform/rise again
7Limbs that can keep acting and reattach even if severed
8Minions (bugs, its own severed hand or floating eye, a crow, etc.)
9Mesmerizing/terrifying gaze
10Aura of cold/heat/smoke/rot/etc.
11Magic, related to how they died
12Shapeshifting (mist/beasts/etc.)

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.KwLjc8KuzLHNd72x]{1d12}Weakness (2)

1Daylight (blinded/burned/fears it)
2Fire (fears it/takes +1d4 damage/etc.)
3Bound to a tether (page 77)
4Hungers for blood/flesh/life/fear/etc.
5Unmistakable odor/aura
6Compulsively counts/cleans/sorts/etc.
7Easily frustrated/confused/provoked
8Burned by salt/rain/moonlight/__
9Repelled by a certain plant/animal/ symbol/etc.
10A taboo (can’t show its face, can’t cross running water, etc.)
11Can’t abide singing/music/laughter
12Burns HP over time/to use its powers
Anan Gllo
Horde, undead, corrupted, tireless,
fearless, terrifying
HP 11; Armor 5 (resilience, armor), 1 vs. bronze or fire
Damage weapon d6 (tags by weapon) or crushing grip d4 (hand, grabby)
Special qualities weaken over time (see below)
Instinct to follow orders
  • Advance implacably
  • Unnerve foes with their silence, their empty eyes
  • Fight on despite crippling injury
The Hillfolk (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.oXGiyvBGsB2GPzQZ]{228}) speak of dark
times, when the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412})
gave a gift to a petty chief. Depending on
who tells the tale, it was a patch of loose
soil or a deep pool of water, the hollow of a
dool tree or a great cauldron of rusted iron.
Regardless: a corpse placed within this ves-
sel would rise as one of the Anan Gllo, the
Empty Dead. The chief raised an undead
army, and set out to make themselves king.
Something interesting: The chief ’s power
was broken when a hero entered the vessel
while still alive, giving their life to destroy
it. A number of Anan Gllo linger, though,
inert and waiting.
Something useful: An Anan Gllo regains
all its HP when it either drinks fresh blood
or re-enters the vessel; it cannot otherwise
regain HP. And every dawn, an Anan Gllo
loses 1 HP. At 0 HP, it becomes inert until
its HP are restored.
Dialeddwr
Solitary, undead, terrifying, implacable
HP 20; Armor 4 (resilience), 0 vs. silver or fire
Damage choking grip or powerful blows d8+2 w/advantage (hand, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities cannot open doors, eat, or drink
Instinct to avenge
  • Pass for alive, except in sunlight
  • Unerringly track a target
  • Lock eyes with someone, show them the meaning of fear
  • Bask in moonlight to regenerate even terrible wounds
Sometimes, when a great wrong is done,
when a good person is cruelly slain, when
the killers go unpunished… well, some-
times they come back. Called back by a
prayer or perhaps a spell. Or sent back
by their fellow victims. Or guided back
by one of the Lady’s own crows. They dig
themselves free of their graves, reborn and
whole. And vengeance shall be theirs.
Pyped
Solitary, undead, terrifying, craven
HP 16; Armor 1 (speed)
Damage bone-snapping grip d8+2 (hand, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities burned by silver (+1d4
damage); harmed by salt (1d8 damage, hand, close); foul stench; sustained by bone marrow
Instinct to avoid the Last Door
  • Move with a surprising, jerky speed
  • Jumble up its memories with those of its host & those whose bones it’s eaten
  • Possess a new corpse, if it can/must
When an ﻿untethered ﻿ghost (page 76) or a
shade (page 85) possesses an empty corpse,
it becomes a pyped. They are wretched
things, all rotting flesh and lurching
movements, hungering for sweet marrow,
scavenging on carrion and graves. But hun-
ger can make them bold. Bold enough to
hunt the weak and sick at the edge of a
village, or a lone traveler caught unawares.
Pypeds rise only from corpses that are
relatively intact, and which have not
undergone proper funeral rites. If a pyped
is destroyed, the spirit within will seek a
new, viable corpse. A shade must find one
before the sun banishes it. An untethered
ghost must find one before its memories
fade and it becomes a wraith (page 79).
Ulliam Unlucky
Solitary, undead, stealthy, terrifying, loyal
HP 16; Armor 0
Damage whatever’s at hand d8 w/disadvantage (tags vary)
Special qualities burned/blinded by daylight; chill aura
Instinct to pine for his family
  • Keep out of sight, or try to
  • Get frustrated, agitated, angry
  • Lose his temper and drain heat from someone, d10 damage w/advantage (hand, close, reach, ignores armor)
Ulliam Unlucky dragged his family from
Marshedge (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eHmYRGms3Q9bzY70]{274}) up to Gordin’s
Delve (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.93L1wwoxn0M4ANCL]{190}), in search of a better
life. But things never did work out. Last
time he went out prospecting, he got lost.
Then the blizzard rolled in. And that’s how
Ulliam died.
But say what you will about Ulliam, he
loved his wife and kids. Still does. Dragged
his frozen self home to see ’em again. He
knows they can’t see him like this, but he
can’t keep himself from lurking. And when
some lech comes sniffing around his wife,
or some fool notices him and makes a
fuss… well, there’s only so much a man can
take. And that little spark of warmth he
draws out of them? Well, he’s starting to
acquire a taste.

Other revenants

  • Mummified Green Lords and their servants (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.gnP4Q10EDePdvgqz]{221})
  • Thralls (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{435}) of the Things Below
  • Nemurvojak (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.CEgJVcGKz5wPubOg]{476})
  • Barrow wights (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{44})
  • Deathless ones (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{46})

Dangers

Hazards

  • Creepy effects: flickering torches, cold spots, things going bump in the night
  • Superstition and fear, affecting the behavior of NPCs
  • Rising dread and terror (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{429}), affecting even PCs
  • Traps (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{42}) placed in old tombs
  • Curses (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.zpb9Ld7t50gAjkx7]{90}) on tombs or tomb-goods
  • Disease (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.805M3qxHZCLZS3Io]{122}), transmitted from fresh or rotting corpses

Other undead

  • Iron hounds (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.7CqkDvDTCiCWTus2]{169})
  • Hollow Fae (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.YMV8KIodnoZL71Jl]{114})
  • A false adept (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DeWQFy1cWoLRvoU5]{329})
Dool spirit
Group, spirit, terrifying, stealthy, devious
HP 13; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage feast on fears d6 w/disadvantage (hand, close, ignores armor)
Special qualities powerless in bright light; bound to its tree or a victim’s bones
Instinct to taste sweet, delicious fears
  • Sense a victim’s doubt and worries
  • Shape sound and shadow to unnerve and frighten
  • Manifest as its victim’s fears (harmed only by one who masters their fear)
  • Bind itself to a victim’s bones
Dool spirits hail from beyond the Last
Door. They are not undead, for they never
lived, but are native to the afterlife. They
sometimes slip into this world, haunting
the dool trees (page 73) from which
they get their name.
When a dool spirit takes a liking to a vic-
tim’s fears, it leaves its tree and binds itself
to that victim’s very bones. It proceeds to
haunt them, and only them, driving them
mad with fear. The spirit remains bound
to that victim’s bones even in death, until
a new dool tree sprouts where those bones
are buried, and the spirit can haunt the
living once more.
Gwyllgi
Solitary, spirit, cautious, stealthy,
terrifying
HP 16; Armor 4 (resilience, 0 vs. silver)
Damage claws d8 (close) or bite d8+2 (hand, grabby, forceful)
Special qualities hates daylight, must be manifest to feed
Instinct to feed on fear and despair
  • Manifest as a black wolf with blazing red eyes
  • Appear to someone out alone at night and lay a death curse on them (see below)
  • Breathe forth a baleful cloud, d6
damage (reach, area, ignores armor) A gwyllgi is a harbinger of death, a being from beyond. A necromancer might call one forth to send against their foes. Or, one might slip through the Last Door when dark magic or the death of many cause it to swing wide open.

Death curse

Threat (affliction) Instinct to crank up tension and dread

  • Cause an accident or coincidence
  • Drive them to anxiety, desperation
  • Push them towards a bad decision
  • Get them, in the end

When a gwyllgi curses a victim, they know they will die before the next full moon.

Something interesting: A gwyllgi can curse only one victim at a time, and it manifests each night to feed on its victim’s emotions.

Something useful: You can lift the curse by destroying the gwyllgi’s form, banishing it back through the Last Door.

Necromancer
Solitary, cautious, cunning, magical,
death-wise
HP 12; Armor 0 (or by worn armor)
Damage silver-alloy knife d8 (hand)
Special qualities can sense active spirits
Instinct to hoard secrets/knowledge
  • Call up, speak with, bind, or lose control of an undead entity
  • Reveal or act on knowledge they should not possess
  • Send the undead against a foe
  • Cast a death-related spell (reload)
‘Tis an odd sort, who would have truck
with spirits of the dead. But some are born
with the Sight, and a morbid curiosity. If
they can avoid getting themselves killed
by hungry ghosts or outraged locals…
well, the dead have all manner of secrets
to teach.
Necromancers often find themselves con-
sorting with entities even darker than the
dead, and are prone to corruption (page
430) by the Things Below. They are likely
to possess one or more arcana (page 508),
which they do not hesitate to use. And
they are often attended by one or more
bound entities.
Shade
Solitary, spirit, undead, cautious
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage none
Special qualities banished by daylight
Instinct (pick 1 or roll 1d6) 1: to refuse to go back, 2: to fulfill its longings, 3-4: to bemoan its fate, 5-6: to return to its rest
  • Manifest as a whisper and a dim image, unable to affect the physical world
  • Share what it knew in life
  • Reveal a secret learned in death
  • “Ride” a willing/prepared host
  • Possess an intact and uninterred corpse, creating a pyped (page 82)
Shades are the souls of the quiet dead,
called back through the Last Door. Or
perhaps they are merely echoes of a dead
person’s personality, given shape by magic.
Either way, they might be called up at the
Crossroads (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}), via an arcanum
like @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.arcana.Item.bs8QvV0vWy2AaSbc]{a strange skull and antlers} (page 520),
or by some other form of necromancy.
A shade can ride a living, willing host, or
one who has been properly prepared (with
arcane markings, by bearing an appropriate
object, via a potion, etc.). It can withstand
daylight while doing so, but only with great
effort. It experiences what its host experi-
ences, and can communicate with its host
via only impulse and desire.
When a shade rides you and compels you to
act against your will, mark XP if you do as it
wants. If you resist, you are Defying Danger.
Tomb-bog spirit
Solitary, spirit, terrifying, implacable
HP 19; Armor 1 (lacks organs)
Damage smothering grip d10+2 (hand, forceful, grabby)
Special qualities harms only those whose life is forfeit
Instinct to consume the dying breaths of the condemned
  • Track down one whose life is forfeit
  • Wait until they are alone
  • Shape a hulking form from peat, soil, and mud (takes half damage from stabbing, slashing, or fire)
  • Whisper its victim’s crimes, freezing them with fear
In Ferrier’s Fen (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.805M3qxHZCLZS3Io]{116}), not far from
Marshedge (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eHmYRGms3Q9bzY70]{274}), there lies a peat
mound where the town buries criminals
alive. Over the years, the place has given
birth to a spirit of death and punishment.
The spirit is young and eager, and can be
set to work upon criminals; see the grim
peat mound (page 511) minor arcanum
for details.
